reference: GATE CS — Digital Logic / Boolean Algebra

map.condition: Count distinct Boolean functions on n variables
Convert SOP to canonical minterms / POS to canonical maxterms

map.use: \text{Apply Boolean identities (De Morgan, absorption, consensus, etc.)}

map.condition: Check if a set of gates is functionally complete

formulas.name: Total Boolean Functions of n Variables
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many Boolean functions can be formed with n variables

terms.means: number of rows in the truth table

formulas.trap: Students confuse 2^n (number of minterms) with 2^(2^n) (number of functions). For n=4: answer is 2^16 = 65536, NOT 16.

formulas.name: Self-Dual Boolean Functions
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many Boolean functions satisfy f(x1,...,xn) = f'(x1',...,xn')

terms.means: half the number of minterms (the other half is determined by duality)

formulas.trap: A self-dual function is NOT the same as its own complement; it maps to the complement when all inputs are complemented.

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.whenToUse: Converting NAND/NOR to AND-OR/OR-AND, or complementing a compound expression
formulas.trap: Applying De Morgan to only part of a complex expression while forgetting to flip AND<->OR throughout the subexpression.

formulas.name: Absorption Laws
formulas.latex: A + AB = A \qquad A(A + B) = A
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying expressions where a variable appears with its own product/sum term
formulas.trap: Confusing A + A'B = A + B (distribution variant) with A + AB = A (absorption). The presence of the complement changes the result.

formulas.name: Consensus Theorem
formulas.plain: AB + A'C + BC = AB + A'C  (BC is redundant)
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminating a redundant term in a sum-of-products to reduce gate count

terms.means: the consensus term, which is covered by AB and A'C together

formulas.trap: Students try to include BC thinking it adds coverage; in Boolean algebra it is already implied and can be dropped.

formulas.name: Complement and Idempotent Laws
formulas.latex: A + A' = 1 \qquad A \cdot A' = 0 \qquad A + A = A \qquad A \cdot A = A
formulas.plain: A + A' = 1 ; A*A' = 0 ; A+A = A ; A*A = A
formulas.whenToUse: Reducing redundant literals or simplifying expressions with repeated or complemented variables
formulas.trap: In Boolean algebra A+A = A, NOT 2A. Treating Boolean variables like integers leads to wrong simplifications.

formulas.name: XOR Definition and Key Properties
formulas.latex: A \oplus B = A'B + AB' \qquad A \oplus A = 0 \qquad A \oplus 0 = A \qquad A \oplus 1 = A'
formulas.plain: A XOR B = A'B + AB' ; A XOR A = 0 ; A XOR 0 = A ; A XOR 1 = A'
formulas.whenToUse: When a * operation or XOR-like definition is given; identifying equivalences
formulas.trap: When given a custom * operator, check if it matches XOR. If x*y = x'y + xy', then x*x = 0, not 1.

formulas.name: Principle of Duality
formulas.plain: Dual of a theorem: swap AND<->OR and 0<->1. If a Boolean identity holds, its dual also holds.
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving a second identity for free once one identity is proven

terms.means: the expression obtained by swapping AND/OR and 0/1

formulas.trap: Duality does NOT complement the variables — only the operators and constants are swapped.

formulas.name: Minterm and Maxterm Definitions
formulas.latex: m_i: \text{product term where all } n \text{ vars appear (uncomplemented if bit=1)} \quad M_i = m_i'
formulas.plain: Minterm m_i: AND of all n literals (unprimed where input bit=1). Maxterm M_i = complement of m_i.
formulas.whenToUse: Expanding any Boolean function to canonical SOP (sum of minterms) or canonical POS (product of maxterms)

terms.means: minterm corresponding to row i of the truth table (f=1)
maxterm corresponding to row i of the truth table (f=0)

formulas.trap: In a maxterm M_i, a variable appears uncomplemented when the corresponding bit is 0 (opposite of minterm convention).

formulas.name: SOP / POS Complement Relationship
formulas.latex: f = \sum m(i_1, i_2, \ldots) \implies f' = \sum m(\text{remaining indices}) = \prod M(i_1, i_2, \ldots)
formulas.plain: If f = SUM(minterms S), then f' = SUM(minterms not in S) = PRODUCT(maxterms in S).
formulas.whenToUse: Finding POS from SOP or complementing a canonical form expression
formulas.trap: The maxterm indices for f are the SAME as the minterm indices where f=0, not where f=1.

formulas.name: XOR of SOP Functions (Minterm Manipulation)
formulas.latex: f_1 \oplus f_2 = \sum m(\text{indices in } f_1 \triangle f_2) \quad (\triangle = \text{symmetric difference})
formulas.plain: f1 XOR f2 = sum of minterms that appear in exactly one of f1, f2 (symmetric difference of their minterm sets).
formulas.whenToUse: Computing output of XOR gate when inputs are given as canonical SOP (sigma notation)

terms.means: symmetric difference of two sets of minterm indices

formulas.trap: AND of two SOP functions = intersection of minterm sets; OR = union; XOR = symmetric difference. Confusing these operations is common.

formulas.name: AND / OR of SOP Functions (Minterm Set Operations)
formulas.latex: f_1 \cdot f_2 = \sum m(S_1 \cap S_2) \qquad f_1 + f_2 = \sum m(S_1 \cup S_2)
formulas.plain: f1 AND f2 = minterms in intersection of their index sets. f1 OR f2 = minterms in union of their index sets.
formulas.whenToUse: Combining functions given in canonical SOP form using AND/OR gates in a circuit

terms.means: sets of minterm indices for f1 and f2

formulas.trap: Only valid when both functions have the SAME number of input variables (same number of bits per minterm index).

formulas.name: CNF / Clause Satisfiability Guarantee
formulas.latex: \text{For any CNF formula with } m \text{ clauses, a truth assignment satisfies} \geq \lceil m/2 \rceil \text{ clauses}
formulas.plain: For any CNF formula with m clauses, there always exists an assignment satisfying at least half (m/2) of the clauses.
formulas.whenToUse: Answering questions about guarantees on clause satisfaction in CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form)

terms.means: number of clauses in the CNF formula
ceiling of m/2, the guaranteed minimum satisfiable clauses

formulas.trap: This guarantee is ALWAYS achievable (by a greedy flip argument), but does not mean ALL clauses are satisfiable (that would be SAT).

formulas.name: Functionally Complete Sets
formulas.plain: {NAND}, {NOR}, {NOT, AND}, {NOT, OR} are each functionally complete sets.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving that all Boolean functions can be realized using only a given gate set
formulas.trap: OR alone is NOT functionally complete (cannot implement NOT). AND alone is NOT functionally complete. {AND, OR} without NOT is also NOT complete.

formulas.name: Deriving NOT / AND / OR from NAND
formulas.latex: \neg A = A \uparrow A \qquad A \land B = (A \uparrow B) \uparrow (A \uparrow B) \qquad A \lor B = (A \uparrow A) \uparrow (B \uparrow B)
formulas.plain: NOT A = NAND(A,A) ; AND(A,B) = NAND(NAND(A,B),NAND(A,B)) ; OR(A,B) = NAND(NOT A, NOT B)
formulas.whenToUse: Showing NAND is functionally complete by constructing NOT, AND, OR from NAND gates
formulas.trap: A NAND gate with one input tied to 1 gives NOT. Students may forget that self-NAND gives NOT.

formulas.name: Deriving NOT / AND / OR from NOR
formulas.latex: \neg A = A \downarrow A \qquad A \lor B = (A \downarrow B) \downarrow (A \downarrow B) \qquad A \land B = (A \downarrow A) \downarrow (B \downarrow B)
formulas.plain: NOT A = NOR(A,A) ; OR(A,B) = NOR(NOR(A,B),NOR(A,B)) ; AND(A,B) = NOR(NOT A, NOT B)
formulas.whenToUse: Showing NOR is functionally complete by constructing NOT, OR, AND from NOR gates
formulas.trap: Symmetric with NAND derivation but the roles of AND/OR are swapped. NOR builds OR first, NAND builds AND first.

formulas.name: Multiplexer as Functionally Complete Element
formulas.latex: \text{A 2:1 MUX (with constant 0 and 1 inputs available) is functionally complete}
formulas.plain: A 2:1 MUX can implement any Boolean function. With S, I0=0, I1=1: output = S (identity). With I0=1,I1=0: output = S' (NOT).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether MUX alone can realize all Boolean functions
formulas.trap: MUX completeness requires access to constants 0 and 1; without them a MUX alone cannot implement NOT.

formulas.name: Half Adder as Functionally Complete Set
formulas.latex: \text{Half adder}: S = A \oplus B,\; C = AB. \text{ Using two HAs and wiring, NOT } A = A \oplus 1 \Rightarrow \text{functionally complete}
formulas.plain: Half adder gives XOR and AND. XOR with input=1 gives NOT. AND+NOT gives NAND, which is complete. So {HA} is functionally complete.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving that any binary function can be implemented using only half adders

terms.means: sum output of half adder (= A XOR B)
carry output of half adder (= A AND B)

formulas.trap: Need to show the path: HA -> XOR+AND -> NOT (via XOR with 1) -> NAND or NOR -> all functions. Skipping the NOT step loses marks.

formulas.name: Post's Functional Completeness Theorem (Criterion)
formulas.latex: \text{A set } F \text{ is NOT complete iff it is a subset of one of: } T_0, T_1, S, M, L
formulas.plain: Post's theorem: a set of functions is functionally complete iff it is NOT a subset of any of the 5 Post classes: T0 (0-preserving), T1 (1-preserving), S (self-dual), M (monotone), L (linear/affine).
formulas.whenToUse: Formally checking whether a given set of Boolean functions is functionally complete
formulas.trap: NAND is not in T0 (NAND(0,0)=1), not in T1 (NAND(1,1)=0), not self-dual, not monotone, not linear — so {NAND} is complete.
